splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on when actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability from multiemployer pension plans must be finalized. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be selected by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that a plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, so the clock stops running during the period of absconding.

circuits.name: Sixth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Barner (6th Cir. precedent)

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to create such a doctrine.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face the stricter gatekeeping requirement of Section 2244(b)(1) — which categorically bars re-raising previously presented claims — when bringing successive collateral attacks under Section 2255. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are barred from raising any previously presented claim, in addition to the separate requirements of Section 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims under § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing a stricter gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners filing § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy the requirements of § 2255(h).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. If the Court adopts broad candidate standing, it would make pre-election challenges to mail-in voting, ballot counting deadlines, and other election rules far easier to bring nationwide. If standing is denied, disputes would be pushed to post-election litigation.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates for federal office have Article III standing to challenge state election laws that allegedly dilute or affect the counting of their votes.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates do not have Article III standing for pre-election challenges to ballot-counting procedures absent a more concrete and particularized injury.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under Title II of the Civil Rights Act?
splits.description: The circuits have applied differing standards for evaluating discrimination claims against hospitality and business operators, particularly regarding proof requirements, available remedies, and the scope of liability. SCOTUS granted cert to resolve the Second Circuit's approach to these legal questions, suggesting a circuit split warranting review.

positions.label: Broader liability standards
positions.summary: Applies a broader standard for establishing discrimination liability in places of public accommodation, with more expansive remedies available to plaintiffs.

positions.label: Narrower liability standards
positions.summary: Applies a more restrictive standard for proving discrimination claims against hospitality establishments, requiring stronger evidence of intentional discrimination.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether courts should apply the rule of lenity or defer to agency/Sentencing Commission interpretations when a criminal sentencing provision is ambiguous. At least three circuits hold that lenity trumps deference, while others (including the Third Circuit panel) have held that deference takes priority. The split has deepened after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes priority over agency or Sentencing Commission deference in interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, resolving ambiguity in the defendant's favor.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference applies even in the criminal context, and the rule of lenity does not override authoritative agency interpretations of ambiguous provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), or whether possessing any one criterion is disqualifying. This split directly affects whether district courts must impose sentences without regard to statutory minimums for large numbers of federal defendants.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they satisfy all three criminal-history criteria, meaning 'and' retains its ordinary conjunctive meaning.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive or distributive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as disjunctive or distributive.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy, based on Application Note 1's commentary, or whether the text unambiguously excludes them.
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds inchoate offenses to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation of the guideline under Stinson/Kisor, or whether the guideline text unambiguously excludes conspiracy and other inchoate offenses. The split affects career-offender sentencing enhancements for many federal defendants.

positions.label: Commentary validly includes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of the career-offender guideline under Stinson and Kisor, and conspiracy qualifies as a controlled substance offense for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Guideline text excludes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously does not include inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline beyond its plain meaning.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether non-retroactive sentencing law changes can serve as a basis for compassionate release. Some circuits hold that such changes categorically cannot constitute extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others permit courts to consider them as one factor. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to resolve this but has itself generated disagreement about the Commission's authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot constitute extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as one factor among others in evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons exist for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an order denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in a federal criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (subject to the 30-day civil appeal deadline) or part of the criminal case (subject to the 14-day criminal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) governs.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Manchester Farming Partnership

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion is part of the criminal case, so the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state controls a substance not listed on federal schedules.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires the substance to be controlled under federal law or whether a state-law offense qualifies regardless. This affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State convictions qualify regardless of federal schedules
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b) broadly covers 'an offense under state law,' and there is no textual basis to require that the substance also be federally controlled.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether property appreciation that occurs after a Chapter 13 filing but before conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that appreciation belongs to the estate, creating a split with circuits that hold the debtor retains post-petition equity increases upon good-faith conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the broad scope of 'property of the estate' under § 541(a) means post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) protects good-faith converting debtors by limiting the estate to the value of property as of the original petition date, meaning post-petition appreciation belongs to the debtor.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging conditions of confinement or BOP decisions affecting the terms (but not the fact or duration) of their imprisonment must proceed under habeas corpus or may bring civil rights or Bivens claims.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for federal prisoners challenging conditions of confinement or BOP administrative decisions, or whether such claims may also proceed under civil rights statutes. The Eighth Circuit acknowledged this unresolved split in the context of First Step Act earned-time-credit disputes.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to conditions of confinement that implicate the duration or execution of a sentence must be brought exclusively through habeas corpus under 28 U.S.C. § 2241.

positions.label: Both habeas and civil rights actions available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights claims (under § 1983 for state prisoners or Bivens for federal prisoners) to challenge conditions of confinement even when habeas relief might also be available.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Carmona v. U.S. Bureau of Prisons

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for the underlying violation of supervised release conditions when imposing a sentence upon revocation, or whether revocation sentences may only serve the limited purpose of sanctioning the breach of trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the permissible purposes of a sentence imposed upon revocation of supervised release. The Sixth Circuit allows courts to punish defendants for the underlying violating conduct, placing it at the extreme of a split with circuits that limit revocation sentences to addressing the breach of the supervisory relationship.

positions.label: Punishment for violation conduct permitted
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for the conduct that violated supervised release conditions when imposing a revocation sentence.

positions.label: Revocation sanctions breach of trust only
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should be limited to sanctioning the breach of the supervisory relationship and the defendant's failure to comply with conditions, not to punish the underlying conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any... broker,' preempts common-law negligence claims against freight brokers for injuries caused by motor carriers they selected. This split affects the liability framework for the freight brokerage industry nationwide.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are 'related to' broker services and thus preempted by the FAAAA's broad preemption clause.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that generally applicable state negligence law is not the type of law 'related to' broker services that Congress intended to preempt, and brokers remain subject to common-law tort liability.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that might also be raised in a habeas petition under § 2255 may be considered as part of the compassionate release analysis. Some circuits allow such consideration as one factor among others, while others hold that habeas-type claims must be raised exclusively through § 2255 and cannot support compassionate release.

positions.label: May consider habeas-type claims in compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider factors related to potential errors in a conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Habeas-type claims cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that claims attacking the validity of a conviction or sentence must be raised through § 2255 and are categorically excluded from the compassionate release analysis.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
